narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the courage it takes to get this far. The admissions model we use predicts whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 64% chance that you would not, which is why your application was rejected—it's a tough threshold designed to ensure student success. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range performance of most students, strongly pushing the model toward expecting bar failure. Your LSAT score of 23.5, well below the average of around 37, was the next biggest factor pulling in the same concerning direction. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.3—lower than the usual 3.2 but still a highlight here—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success. Similarly, your low family income, while a real hurdle in your journey, actually contributed positively to the model's view, perhaps reflecting resilience in overcoming obstacles that many don't face. Your Year 1 decile in the bottom group added some additional weight toward the failure prediction, though less than the top factors, while your choice of full-time study aligned with the norm for most students and offered a small boost. Compared to typical applicants, who often have stronger early and later academic deciles, higher test scores, and similar backgrounds, your profile stood out as higher risk in those key academic areas despite your strengths elsewhere. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've navigated as a Black woman from a low-income background—it's just the model's data-driven assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional courses, and highlighting any resilience-building experiences in future applications. Many students in similar situations have improved their profiles and succeeded—keep pushing, and reach out to advisors for personalized strategies. You've got this potential; now it's about refining those key areas.
